steps.title: Identify the grammatical structures
steps.content: Before the blank: “The sand at Balos Beach in Greece is a more unusual shade” — an independent clause with a complete subject and verb. After the blank: “deposits of crushed coral and other organic matter lend the sand a unique pink hue” — another complete independent clause. The word “though” will join them as a conjunctive adverb, expressing a relationship (elaboration or subtle contrast) between two related ideas.

steps.title: Apply the rule for two independent clauses with a conjunctive adverb
steps.content:
Two independent clauses joined by a conjunctive adverb require a semicolon to mark the boundary between them. The pattern here is comma (linking the adverb to the first clause), the transition word “though,” then a semicolon (separating the two main ideas): IC, though; IC. This punctuation properly distinguishes the two complete thoughts while signaling their connection through “though.”

The other choices fail: A uses only a comma between two ICs (a comma splice), B incorrectly uses a colon before “though” (colons don't introduce transitions this way), and C sets “though” as a parenthetical element with double commas, breaking the flow between the related clauses.

That matches choice D — D is correct.
